item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Given your explanation, I think I agree with you. The whole "antimemetic-but-not-amnestic" territory probably has a lot of untapped potential, and it's cool to see someone exploring that space. Nice work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, it's actually interesting that you asked this, because it's something I've thought about a lot before and haven't gotten the opportunity to pontificate about.

+ Antimeme?

So, this SCP was directly inspired by We Need To Talk About Fifty-Five and the Antimemetics Division Hub. In fact, it's inspired by the very first line of the hub:

An antimeme is *an idea with self-censoring properties*; an idea which, by its intrinsic nature, discourages or prevents people from spreading it.

Now, the overwhelming majority of antimemetic articles on the site are those which deal with memory - anomalous effects which prevent your ability to recall them, and then act on them - but whenever qntm defined an antimeme in the original series of the Antimemetics Division, the definition often had little to do with memory and follow the above - the idea which cannot, for whatever reason, be communicated. I believe he once called 2521 antimemetic.

So, I was rather … intrigued by the lack of antimemetic content outside of memory stuff. Everything tends towards that one concept, which has a lot of room and is cool. But the definition implies for a much broader definition, one that includes stuff like this. The incommunicability of the memory stuff is a consequence of forgetting, rather than the primary anomalous effect.

That's fundamentally what inspired this SCP, at least the anomaly. With that in mind, I felt like I couldn't not call it an antimeme: it's a linear evolution from the definition.

Does it fit the general conception of antimeme on the site? Not really, no. That is the case. It's a very different concept, but it is designed to fall under the given definition.

It's also worth noting that, of course, it's not an antimeme, but a novel antimeme, in that it is unique and unusual. There is an acknowledgment it is not a traditional, memory-related antimeme. The mechanisms by which it inhibits memetic spread are not the normal type of antimeme. It's also not handled by the Antimemetics Division but the Department of Miscommunications, since it does slot into their wheelhouse better.

TL;DR: I say yes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Does this actually qualify as an antimeme? My understanding was that antimemes cause those affected by them to forget about the antimeme itself. In this case, it seems like those affected can still remember the specifics of the antimeme. Mnestics, the typical counter to antimemes, would be useless here, because remembering the anomaly doesn't help you reveal its properties. This seems more like a cognitohazard whose hazardous property just happens to be suppression of the ability/desire to communicate about the hazard itself.

It kinda makes me want to explore the idea of a cognitohazard whose hazard is that it forces those affected to act as if they don't remember the hazard, even if they do. The antimemetics division would probably have a hard time with that one, too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this. I don't know if it's quite what was intended, but my first thought after what turned out to be the "correct" interpretation was actually the way it's common to avoid outright referring to some serial killers etc. by name so as to avoid romanticizing them/encouraging any copycats. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Edit: sorry, managed to put this in the wrong place…

item.title: Re: Your Dirty Little Secret
item.content:encoded:
spoilers
– hide block

Urinal pooper, got it.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This feels like trying to plant a seed in well-tread ground to me. Like ARD said, this is reminiscent of SCP-3935, and it also felt like SCP-3855 and a couple of other SCPs whose numbers escape me at the moment. It doesn't bring anything new to the table for me, and I wasn't able to get into it.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it but I don't love it. I feel like this sets itself up for an accompanying tale and exploring the mystery behind this small town would greatly improve how I feel about this piece. As it stands alone though it's still a +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: After reading the explanation above, I actually think making the actual event more interesting and more central to the story would help. This is a really good idea but unfortunately there's not a lot of meat on the bones, so to speak. Still, being from a small town, this resonated, and I found it intriguing enough to spare an upvote.

item.title: Re: Your Dirty Little Secret
item.content:encoded: This take genuinely caused me to rethink the piece and decide to upvote, so thank you for the insight. A small town secret becoming so ingrained that it becomes anomalous isn't completely unheard of, but the subtlety of it warrants appreciation. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't think there is a lot of fertile ground left for the whole "antimeme" thing but this article manages to provide the necessary subtlety in order to convey that there is a lot more going on here and the fact that it's not/can't be talked about is attractive for this piece.

This was enough content in a small enough space that I found it worth an upvote, but I feel that this would benefit from perhaps making the implications a little more profound (while preserving the vague). I know the foundation deemed this anomalous for reasons you stated, but to me there is an unstated suggestion here that it really might just not be. I like that. If that's me putting words into your mouth that you didn't intend, such is the beauty of the art of writing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is one of those "quietly good" articles. It never hits you with anything. There isn't a big progression, really. It's just a concept presented as such that is nice and simple and good. Kind of seems to be the Department of Miscommunication's M.O. at the moment. I'm all for it. There is something unique about reading something, and not having a big huge reaction to it, and just going, "hey, nice, that was good". This hits that "sipping tea and reading skips with not a care in the world" vibe. I dunno if what I'm saying makes any sense, but yea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a idea with a lot of potential that ultimately doesn't do enough with its core themes to be substantially meaningful or atmospheric, especially compared to SCP-3935 which mines the same vein of small-town horror and tragedy to much greater effect.

item.title: Your Dirty Little Secret
item.content:encoded:
Thanks to Rounderhouse, DrChandra, DarkStuff and RadioactiveRads for looking at this.

So there's two layers to this: what happened, and how the people of the town reacted. The second is directly connected to the anomaly, and the first is incidental. I have a headcanon in mind for what the first is, but it's something that I don't plan to reveal. As for the second….

spoilers
– hide block

Callam Township is a small town, where something very bad (abuse, murder, drugs — something scandalous) happened, and a lot of people got together to coverup, and all agreed to never talk about it again. Their deep-held devotion to that idea of not talking about it caused it to become anomalous, they kept refusing to talk until nobody could talk, even those who weren't in on it.

The actual event isn't as important as the reaction.
